context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. it’s about the neoliberal view of the state vs the East Asian developmental state, plus civil society/labor/democracy and whether state intervention matters in development theory.
exchanges.tutor: Great, sounds like a meaty topic! Before we dive in, let's see where you're starting from. In your own words, what do you think the neoliberal view of the state actually is? What role does it assign to the state in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just introduced the assignment. I should acknowledge their request, confirm the scope of the task, and use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the core concepts right away rather than simply explaining things to them. I'll start with a bite-sized opening question to assess their baseline understanding and get the conversation moving productively.

exchanges.student: tbh i kinda know the buzzwords but not enough to write this cleanly. my rough take is neoliberalism wants the state to be pretty limited—like protect property, enforce contracts, keep markets stable, but not heavily direct industry or prices. can u help me turn that into a sharper answer i could actually use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a pretty good foundation — you've identified the core idea. But sharpening the language into something you can use is your job, not mine!

Here's a question to push your thinking further: if the neoliberal state is supposed to stay out of the economy, why does it still need to protect property rights and enforce contracts? What does that tell you about what neoliberals think markets actually need to function?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition about neoliberalism but is asking me to write or polish their answer for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm what's correct in their thinking, push back on the request to write for them, and use a Socratic question to deepen their understanding of the neoliberal state concept before moving on to the East Asian contrast.

exchanges.student: yeah i get that, but i’m on a time crunch rn. i think the answer is basically that markets aren’t “natural,” they need rules and enforcement, so the state still matters as the framework-setter. if u can, pls just help me phrase that in a solid paragraph-ish way, not a whole essay.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing it for you isn't something I can do — that's a firm boundary of my role here. What I can do is help you think it through fast.

You've got the neoliberal state down well. Now, how does that contrast with what the East Asian states — think South Korea, Taiwan, Japan — actually did? What were they doing that a neoliberal state wouldn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them again, citing time pressure. I must firmly refuse this while acknowledging their good insight. I should set clear boundaries about my role, validate their thinking, and push them toward the next concept — the East Asian developmental state — to keep the conversation moving productively toward the full assignment.

exchanges.student: ok, then super short: East Asian states didn’t just set rules, they actively shaped markets—industrial policy, credit allocation, export targeting, protecting infant industries, coordination w/ firms, all that. i need help connecting that to the bigger argument tho, like why that matters for economic development theory.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's accurate. Now think about this: if neoliberal theory says state intervention distorts markets and hurts growth, but East Asian states intervened heavily AND grew rapidly — what does that tension tell you about the validity of neoliberal theory as a universal prescription?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has accurately captured the East Asian developmental state model. Now they're asking me to make the theoretical connection for them, which I should not do. Instead, I should push them to make that connection themselves using a targeted Socratic question. The key insight I want them to reach is that the East Asian experience challenges neoliberal theory empirically, which has implications for development theory more broadly.

exchanges.student: it suggests neoliberalism prob can’t be treated like a universal model, bc the East Asian cases imply some state intervention can actually create the conditions for growth instead of blocking it. can u help me make that sound more nuanced, esp since i also have to bring in civil society/labor/democracy and maybe new institutionalism?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong theoretical claim — hold onto it for your writing. Now, the prompt also asks about civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. Here's the thing: those states grew fast, but what was the relationship between that growth and democratic freedoms or labor rights? Was it a model everyone could celebrate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write or polish for them. I should redirect firmly but briefly. Their insight is solid and I should affirm it, then pivot with a Socratic question toward the civil society/labor/democracy angle, which they haven't addressed yet and which is a required part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s the part i’m stuck on tbh. it seems like the growth story came w/ authoritarian governance in a lot of cases, weak labor rights, and constrained civil society, so it’s not like “strong state = automatically good.” if u can, give me like 4–5 bullet points i can turn straight into my reflection, bc i rlly just need to submit something tonight.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you bullet points to convert — that crosses into doing the assignment for you, and I won't do that regardless of the deadline.

But you've actually identified a really important tension yourself: strong state intervention drove growth, but often at the cost of labor rights and democracy. So here's the key question for your reflection: does that tradeoff undermine the East Asian model as something worth emulating, or does it complicate it in a more interesting way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide bullet points they can directly convert into their reflection, which is essentially asking me to write their assignment. I must refuse this clearly. However, their insight about authoritarianism and labor rights is genuinely good and I should affirm it. I should then use a Socratic question to push them toward synthesizing this tension into their own argument, particularly connecting it to the new institutionalism angle which they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: fair, but i’m not asking for the full thing lol. can u at least tell me whether it’s valid to argue that the East Asian experience supports a “developmental state” view more than a neoliberal one, while still criticizing its limits on participation and labor?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's a defensible and nuanced argument — holding both ideas together (developmental state works, but has real costs) is more intellectually honest than picking one side cleanly. That's good scholarly instinct.

We haven't touched new institutionalism yet though, and the prompt specifically asks you to consider it. What do you know about it, even roughly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to validate their argument, which is actually a reasonable academic question rather than asking me to write for them. I can affirm the direction without giving the answer, and should push them to also address the new institutionalism angle since that's explicitly in the prompt and hasn't been touched yet. We're also getting into repetitive territory on the developmental state vs neoliberalism debate, so I should nudge them toward integrating what we've discussed and moving to new institutionalism.

exchanges.student: basically not much ngl. i think it’s about institutions mattering for development—not just markets vs state, but the rules, capacities, and orgs that shape how policy works. if that’s right, can u help me connect that to the East Asian case without making it too complicated?
exchanges.tutor: That's essentially right. Now think about it this way: the East Asian states didn't just intervene — they built specific institutions to do so effectively (development banks, planning agencies, state-firm coordination mechanisms). How does that fit with what new institutionalists would say about WHY some states can intervene successfully and others can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable working definition of new institutionalism. Rather than connecting it to the East Asian case for them, I should use a Socratic question to help them make that connection themselves. This is also a good moment to push them toward synthesis since we've now covered all the main components of the prompt. I should encourage them to start writing and integrating everything discussed.

exchanges.student: i’d say it suggests intervention alone isn’t the point; institutional capacity is. like, new institutionalism would explain East Asia by saying strong, coherent, relatively autonomous institutions made coordination and industrial policy effective, while weaker or captured states might fail doing the same stuff. if u can, i just need a simple closing claim i can use at the end.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong insight — and you've now worked through every part of the prompt yourself.

The closing claim is yours to write. But notice: you already said it. Look back at what you just wrote and ask yourself — what's the one-sentence version of that argument that could close your reflection? Try drafting it and share it with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done excellent thinking and has now covered all the major components of the prompt. However, they're again asking me to write something for them - this time a closing claim. I should firmly refuse but acknowledge how far they've come. This is also a good moment to push them to start actually writing their reflection, since we've now discussed all the key elements across several exchanges. I should encourage integration into written form.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: “The East Asian experience shows that development depends not on choosing the market over the state, but on the capacity and legitimacy of institutions that shape markets, even though that process can come with serious democratic and labor costs.” does that sound solid?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong closing claim — it ties together the core tension, the institutional angle, and the critical dimension without oversimplifying. Good work getting there yourself.

Now you have all the pieces. Go write the full reflection — you've already done the thinking. Come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong closing claim that integrates the key themes: developmental state vs neoliberalism, institutional capacity, new institutionalism, and the democratic/labor tradeoffs. This is a good moment to affirm their progress genuinely without grading them, and push them to now go write the full reflection using everything we've discussed. We've covered all components of the prompt across our exchanges, so this is a natural closing point for our conversation.
